signals.title: NZD/CAD — G8 Bilateral Theme + Geopolitical Shift
signals.text: NZD/CAD at 0.8190 — NZD outperformance (1W avg +0.931%) vs CAD weakness (-0.605%) creates a 1.536% G8 spread, the widest in the complex. Peace hopes in the Middle East drive a -3.43% drop in WTI Oil ($91.8), stripping the CAD of its energy-linked risk premium while boosting high-beta NZD risk appetite. RBNZ hike bias (2.25%, market bias: Hike) vs BoC hold (2.25%) provides a structural tailwind for NZD as oil-driven CAD selling accelerates. Implication: NZD/CAD remains bid as long as oil prices retreat and risk-on sentiment persists.

signals.title: AUD/USD — Positioning + Retail Divergence
signals.text: AUD/USD at 0.7260 — COT LF net long +48,299 (74.8% long) vs retail 90% short. Institutional positioning is heavily long while retail is aggressively fading the 1M +5.43% rally. RBA 4.35% vs Fed 3.75% provides a +60bp carry advantage for AUD, further supported by the risk-on shift following US-Iran peace headlines. Implication: Extreme retail short positioning suggests a capitulation risk that could fuel a squeeze toward 0.7350 if price holds above 0.7200.

signals.title: EUR/USD — Correlation Break + Geopolitical Reversal
signals.text: EUR/USD at 1.1774 — EUR/USD vs EuroStoxx correlation at +0.729 vs norm of -0.130 (z=+1.84σ). The pair is decoupling from traditional equity drivers, instead tracking the reversal of war-related risk premiums. Fed 3.75% vs ECB 2.0% maintains a 175bp USD carry advantage, but DXY weakness (-0.16%) on peace hopes allows EUR to test upside levels. Implication: EUR/USD strength is a function of USD liquidation rather than EUR conviction; watch for 1.1800 resistance to hold unless ECB bias shifts hawkish.

signals.title: USD/JPY — Carry vs Intervention Risk
signals.text: USD/JPY at 156.38 — Fed 3.75% vs BoJ 0.75% provides a 300bp carry premium supporting the USD. JPY COT LF net short -88,543 (30.6% long) remains crowded, but the MOVE index drop (-8.00% to 70.6) suggests reduced volatility stress. 160.00 remains the key level for BoJ verbal warnings; intervention risk rises on a sustained break above that threshold. Implication: Carry remains the dominant driver while VIX stays at 17.4 (normal volatility), keeping the pair bid on dips.
